aiMessages.content:
Created a new grammY Telegram bot project with MongoDB persistence for chat settings (rules/welcome), FAQ entries, suggestions, warn tracking, and audit logs. Implemented /start with buttons, /help with categorized commands, welcome-on-join behavior with placeholders, admin-only commands with chat-admin checks plus BOT_ADMIN_IDS override, and a prompt-next-message pattern via a pending-input store. Added long polling via @grammyjs/runner with webhook clearing, 409 backoff, and production-safe logs. DOCS.md and .env.sample are included.

planMarkdown:
Bot: Community Builder Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that helps people start, run, and grow communities inside Telegram groups. It focuses on onboarding new members, establishing rules and roles, capturing introductions, creating lightweight engagement loops (prompts, polls), and providing moderators with clear tooling for content management and member support.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence. It supports multi-group operation, per-group configuration, and robust moderation workflows while keeping privacy, rate limits, and operational reliability in mind.

Product goals:
Primary goals
1) Make it easy to launch a community group: rules, welcome message, onboarding steps, and member introductions.
2) Improve retention and engagement: prompts, recurring posts, polls, simple events, and member discovery.
3) Reduce moderator workload: spam/raid mitigation, reporting, queueing items for review, and quick actions.
4) Provide community health visibility: basic analytics like member join trends, active members, top contributors, and onboarding completion.

Non-goals (initial scope control)
1) Not a full CRM or newsletter platform.
2) Not a marketplace or payments system.
3) Not cross-platform; Telegram-only.

Target users and roles
1) Owners/admins: configure community settings and policies.
2) Moderators: handle reports, approvals, and content actions.
3) Members: onboard, introduce themselves, participate in prompts, and discover community resources.

Success metrics
1) Onboarding completion rate (e.g., introductions posted, rules acknowledged).
2) Moderator time saved (e.g., fewer manual welcomes, fewer spam messages remaining).
3) Engagement lift (responses to prompts/polls, event RSVPs).
4) Reduced spam incidents and faster response time to reports.

High-level architecture:
High-level design
1) Single Node.js service/process running grammY.
2) Webhook or long polling depending on deployment preference; design should support either.
3) MongoDB as the system of record for configuration, members, events, and moderation logs.
4) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for media assets used in welcome posts, templates, and exports (if needed).

Core modules (logical boundaries)
1) Bot runtime
   1) grammY bot instance with middleware pipeline.
   2) Update router by chat type (private vs group/supergroup) and by feature area.
2) Identity and permissions
   1) Resolve Telegram user and chat identities.
   2) Admin/mod role checks using Telegram chat member status plus optional bot-managed role overlays.
3) Community configuration
   1) Per-group settings: welcome templates, rules, onboarding flow, moderation thresholds, feature toggles.
4) Onboarding and engagement
   1) Join flow: greet, rules acknowledgement, intro prompts.
   2) Scheduled prompts and announcements driven by a simple in-process scheduler backed by MongoDB.
5) Moderation and safety
   1) Anti-spam heuristics, raid mode, link restrictions.
   2) Member reports and moderator review queue.
   3) Action logging (who did what, when, and why).
6) Observability and operations
   1) Structured logging with correlation identifiers (chat id, user id, update id).
   2) Error tracking strategy (log aggregation in deployment environment).
   3) Admin diagnostics commands.

Update handling and resilience
1) Idempotency: store processed update ids per bot instance runtime window and/or persist recent update ids per chat to reduce duplicated side effects.
2) Rate limiting: per-chat and per-user throttles to prevent command spam and avoid Telegram API limits.
3) Retries: for transient Telegram API failures with bounded retry policies and clear user feedback when needed.
4) Graceful degradation: if MongoDB is unavailable, the bot should still avoid destructive actions and respond with a temporary service message to admins.

Scheduling approach (single process constraint)
1) Use an in-process scheduler loop that reads due jobs from MongoDB (nextRunAt <= now).
2) Jobs must be idempotent and include a lock with short TTL semantics in MongoDB to avoid duplicate sends in case of restart.
3) Keep jobs small: send message, update nextRunAt, and record outcome.

Privacy boundaries
1) Avoid storing message content unless necessary (e.g., introductions, reports). Prefer storing message ids and metadata.
2) Provide per-community retention settings for logs and member data.
3) Provide export and deletion workflows for admins.

Data model:
Collections overview (MongoDB)
1) communities
   1) chatId (unique), type, title
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) settings: welcomeEnabled, welcomeTemplate, rulesText, rulesMessageId, introEnabled, introPrompt, language, timezone, featureFlags
   4) moderation: raidMode, linkPolicy, spamSensitivity, minAccountAgeDays, verificationMode
   5) roles: adminOverrides (optional), moderatorUserIds (optional)

2) users
   1) userId (unique), username, firstName, lastName
   2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
   3) globalFlags: isBannedGlobal (optional)

3) communitymembers
   1) communityChatId + userId (compound unique)
   2) joinedAt, leftAt (optional)
   3) onboarding: rulesAcceptedAt, introMessageId, introText (optional), onboardingState
   4) moderation: warningsCount, mutedUntil (optional), notes (optional)
   5) activity: lastActiveAt, messageCountApprox (optional)

4) moderationactions
   1) communityChatId, actorUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) actionType (warn, mute, kick, ban, deletemessage, approve, reject)
   3) reason, metadata (messageId, evidenceIds)
   4) createdAt

5) reports
   1) communityChatId, reporterUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) messageId (optional), messageLink (optional if available)
   3) category, description
   4) status (open, inreview, resolved, dismissed)
   5) assignedToUserId (optional), resolutionNote (optional)
   6) createdAt, updatedAt

6) scheduledjobs
   1) communityChatId
   2) jobType (prompt, announcement, poll)
   3) payload (template id, text, options)
   4) schedule: cronLike (optional), intervalSeconds (optional), nextRunAt
   5) lock: lockedUntil, lockedBy
   6) lastRunAt, lastResult (success/failure), failureCount

7) contenttemplates (optional)
   1) templateId, communityChatId (optional for global templates)
   2) type (welcome, prompt, rules)
   3) text, mediaRef (optional)
   4) createdAt, updatedAt

Indexes and retention
1) Ensure compound indexes for communitymembers and time-based indexes for moderationactions, reports, and scheduledjobs.
2) Use TTL indexes for ephemeral data (e.g., job locks, transient verification tokens) and optionally for moderation logs based on community retention configuration.

Object/file storage references
1) Store only references in MongoDB (mediaRef) pointing to object storage or filesystem storage paths.
2) Keep media optional; most features can work text-first.

Key flows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin adds bot to a group and grants required permissions (delete messages, ban users, pin messages if needed).
2) Admin runs /setup in the group.
3) Bot verifies admin status via Telegram chat member status.
4) Bot guides configuration via an interactive flow:
   1) Set welcome message
   2) Set rules text (and optionally pin rules)
   3) Enable introductions and choose intro prompt
   4) Configure moderation defaults (raid mode toggle, link policy)
   5) Enable engagement prompts and choose schedule
5) Bot saves community settings in MongoDB and posts a confirmation summary.

2) Member joins onboarding
1) Bot receives newchatmembers event.
2) If welcome is enabled, bot posts a welcome message and a short CTA:
   1) Read rules
   2) Introduce yourself
3) If verificationMode is enabled, bot may restrict new members temporarily (based on available Telegram permissions) and lift restrictions after a successful interaction.
4) Bot records join event and onboarding state in communitymembers.

3) Rules acknowledgement
1) Member clicks an inline button or uses a private chat command to acknowledge rules.
2) Bot records rulesAcceptedAt.
3) Bot optionally assigns a visible role label through messaging (Telegram does not support custom roles natively) and may lift temporary restrictions.

4) Introductions
1) Bot prompts the member to post an introduction in the group (or collects it in private and reposts to the group with consent).
2) Bot stores intro message id and optional intro text (if configured).
3) Bot marks onboarding complete and optionally sends a friendly follow-up with next steps (resources, channels, weekly prompt schedule).

5) Engagement prompts and polls
1) Admin configures recurring prompts via /prompts.
2) Scheduler picks due jobs and posts prompts/polls into the group.
3) Bot tracks basic engagement metrics: count of replies or poll participation.
4) Admin can pause, reschedule, or post ad-hoc prompts.

6) Reporting and moderation queue
1) Members report content via /report (reply to a message) or via an inline button placed on selected bot posts.
2) Bot creates a report record, acknowledges the reporter, and notifies moderators in a dedicated mod chat (optional) or via direct messages.
3) Moderators review and take action through buttons (delete, warn, mute, ban) depending on bot permissions.
4) Bot executes the action, logs it in moderationactions, and resolves the report.

7) Anti-spam and raid mode
1) Bot applies heuristics on messages from new members:
   1) Too many links
   2) Repeated text
   3) Suspicious account age thresholds (where feasible)
2) If triggered, bot can delete the message and create an internal moderation event.
3) In raid mode, bot applies stricter thresholds and may temporarily limit posting for new joins.
4) Provide clear moderator overrides and audit trail.

8) Admin analytics and health checks
1) /stats shows onboarding completion, join/leave trends, reports volume, moderation actions, and engagement.
2) /diagnostics runs permission checks and highlights misconfiguration (missing delete permission, cannot restrict members, etc.).

9) Data deletion and export
1) /privacy shows what is stored and for how long.
2) Admin can request /export for community configuration and summary stats.
3) Admin can run /purgeoldlogs with a retention window.
4) Respect user deletion requests where applicable by removing user profile fields and keeping only aggregated counts if needed.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, middleware, and conversation-style flows.
3) Environment configuration via process environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for community settings, member states, moderation logs, reports, and scheduled jobs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caches only (rate limit counters, temporary interaction state), with safe fallbacks after restart.

Storage
1) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for optional media assets, exports, and template attachments.
2) MongoDB stores only references/paths and metadata for stored objects.

Scheduling and background work
1) In-process scheduler that polls MongoDB for due jobs and uses MongoDB-based locking to avoid duplicates.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Operations
1) Webhook or long polling; choose based on deployment environment constraints.
2) Structured logs written to stdout/stderr for collection by the hosting environment.
3) Basic health endpoint (if using webhooks) to support uptime checks and to confirm MongoDB connectivity.

Security
1) Secrets stored only in environment variables.
2) Strict validation of callback data and command inputs.
3) Permission checks for all admin/moderator actions against Telegram chat roles and community settings.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent handlers for join events, scheduled sends, and moderation actions.
2) Defensive handling for missing permissions and Telegram API errors.
3) Safe defaults: do not delete user content unless rules are clearly configured and bot permissions are verified.

Performance
1) Keep hot-path operations O(1) per update: minimal DB queries, use indexed lookups.
2) Use in-memory rate limiters to reduce repeated DB reads.
3) Batch analytics updates (e.g., update counters periodically) rather than per message if needed.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Rate limit commands per user and per chat.
2) Validate that callback queries originate from the same community context.
3) Maintain an audit trail for moderator actions.
4) Provide a global allowlist for bot owners to access sensitive diagnostics.

Privacy and compliance posture
1) Data minimization: store only what is required for features.
2) Configurable retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.
3) Clear transparency via /privacy command.
4) Allow per-user data deletion requests where feasible.

Maintainability
1) Modular design with feature flags per community.
2) Versioned settings schema with migrations for MongoDB documents.
3) Clear separation of concerns: configuration, onboarding, moderation, engagement, analytics.

Operational readiness
1) Startup checks: verify TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists and MongoDB is reachable.
2) Admin-facing diagnostics to confirm permissions and configuration.
3) Clear runbooks for common failures: missing permissions, webhook misconfiguration, MongoDB downtime.

Internationalization readiness (optional)
1) Store a per-community language setting.
2) Keep all user-visible strings in a message catalog for future localization.

Bot commands:
• /start — Start the bot in private chat and see available features and privacy info. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show help and links to setup steps for admins and usage tips for members. (usage: /help) [General]
• /setup — Interactive community setup wizard for a group/supergroup. (usage: /setup (run in the group by an admin)) [Admin]
• /settings — View and edit community settings (welcome, rules, intros, moderation). (usage: /settings (group admin only)) [Admin]
• /rules — Show the community rules and how to acknowledge them. (usage: /rules) [Community]
• /introduce — Post an introduction prompt and guide the user through introduction steps. (usage: /introduce (member) or /introduce @user (mod/admin)) [Community]
• /prompts — Manage recurring engagement prompts and polls. (usage: /prompts (admin only)) [Admin]
• /prompt — Send an ad-hoc prompt to spark discussion. (usage: /prompt Your question here (admin/mod only)) [Moderation]
• /report — Report a message to moderators (use as a reply). (usage: /report (reply to a message) [optional reason]) [Safety]
• /modqueue — View open reports and moderation items. (usage: /modqueue (mod/admin only)) [Moderation]
• /warn — Warn a user (reply to a message or specify user). (usage: /warn (reply) [reason] or /warn @user [reason]) [Moderation]
• /mute — Temporarily mute a user (requires restrict permissions). (usage: /mute (reply) 30m [reason] or /mute @user 2h [reason]) [Moderation]
• /ban — Ban a user from the community (requires ban permissions). (usage: /ban (reply) [reason] or /ban @user [reason]) [Moderation]
• /raidmode — Toggle stricter anti-spam controls during raids. (usage: /raidmode on|off (admin only)) [Safety]
• /stats — Show community health metrics and onboarding/engagement summaries. (usage: /stats (admin/mod only)) [Analytics]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored and offer admin/user data actions. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /export — Export community configuration and summary stats for admins. (usage: /export (admin only)) [Admin]
• /diagnostics — Check bot permissions, configuration health, and connectivity status. (usage: /diagnostics (admin only)) [Admin]

1) /privacy shows what is stored and for how long.
2) Admin can request /export for community configuration and summary stats.
3) Admin can run /purgeoldlogs with a retention window.
4) Respect user deletion requests where applicable by removing user profile fields and keeping only aggregated counts if needed.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, middleware, and conversation-style flows.
3) Environment configuration via process environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for community settings, member states, moderation logs, reports, and scheduled jobs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caches only (rate limit counters, temporary interaction state), with safe fallbacks after restart.

Storage
1) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for optional media assets, exports, and template attachments.
2) MongoDB stores only references/paths and metadata for stored objects.

Scheduling and background work
1) In-process scheduler that polls MongoDB for due jobs and uses MongoDB-based locking to avoid duplicates.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Operations
1) Webhook or long polling; choose based on deployment environment constraints.
2) Structured logs written to stdout/stderr for collection by the hosting environment.
3) Basic health endpoint (if using webhooks) to support uptime checks and to confirm MongoDB connectivity.

Security
1) Secrets stored only in environment variables.
2) Strict validation of callback data and command inputs.
3) Permission checks for all admin/moderator actions against Telegram chat roles and community settings.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent handlers for join events, scheduled sends, and moderation actions.
2) Defensive handling for missing permissions and Telegram API errors.
3) Safe defaults: do not delete user content unless rules are clearly configured and bot permissions are verified.

Performance
1) Keep hot-path operations O(1) per update: minimal DB queries, use indexed lookups.
2) Use in-memory rate limiters to reduce repeated DB reads.
3) Batch analytics updates (e.g., update counters periodically) rather than per message if needed.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Rate limit commands per user and per chat.
2) Validate that callback queries originate from the same community context.
3) Maintain an audit trail for moderator actions.
4) Provide a global allowlist for bot owners to access sensitive diagnostics.

Privacy and compliance posture
1) Data minimization: store only what is required for features.
2) Configurable retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.
3) Clear transparency via /privacy command.
4) Allow per-user data deletion requests where feasible.

Maintainability
1) Modular design with feature flags per community.
2) Versioned settings schema with migrations for MongoDB documents.
3) Clear separation of concerns: configuration, onboarding, moderation, engagement, analytics.

Operational readiness
1) Startup checks: verify TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists and MongoDB is reachable.
2) Admin-facing diagnostics to confirm permissions and configuration.
3) Clear runbooks for common failures: missing permissions, webhook misconfiguration, MongoDB downtime.

Internationalization readiness (optional)
1) Store a per-community language setting.
2) Keep all user-visible strings in a message catalog for future localization.

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot helps communities onboard members and gives admins lightweight management tools.

Behavior by chat type
1) Private chat (DM)
- Member tools: view rules, browse/search FAQ, send suggestions.
- Admin tools: manage FAQs, review suggestions, send announcements to configured target chats.

2) Group/supergroup
- Automatically welcomes new members if welcome is enabled for that chat.
- Admin tools: set rules, set welcome template, toggle welcome, moderation warnings, announcements, audit log.

Public commands
1) /start
- In private: shows welcome message and buttons for Rules, FAQ, Suggestion, Help.
- In groups: tells users to DM the bot for member tools.

2) /help
- Shows categorized commands for members and admins.

Member commands (private)
1) /rules
Shows rules for your current community context.

2) /faq
Shows FAQ list or searches when you provide terms.
Examples:
- /faq
- /faq wallet setup

3) /suggest
Prompts you to send a suggestion as your next message.

Admin commands (groups and private)
1) /admin
Shows admin panel summary for the current chat (in groups) or asks you to select a target chat (in private).

2) /setrules <text>
Sets rules text for the chat. If you omit <text>, the bot will ask you to send the next message as rules within 60 seconds.

3) /setwelcome <template>
Sets welcome message template for the chat. Supports placeholders: {first_name}, {username}, {chat_title}.
If you omit <template>, the bot will ask for the next message within 60 seconds.

4) /welcome_on and /welcome_off
Enable or disable welcome messages for the chat.

5) /faq_add <question> | <answer>
Adds an FAQ entry.

6) /faq_edit <id> <question> | <answer>
Edits an FAQ entry by id.

7) /faq_del <id>
Deletes an FAQ entry.

8) /suggestions
Lists recent suggestions for the current target chat.

9) /suggest_set <id> <new|accepted|rejected|planned|done>
Updates suggestion status.

10) /announce
Guided announcement flow: choose target(s), enter text, confirm send.

11) /announce_targets
Manage announcement target chats (add/remove) for your admin account.

12) /warn (reply)
Increments warn count for the replied user in that chat.

13) /warns (reply)
Shows warn count for the replied user.

14) /resetwarns (reply)
Resets warn count.

15) /audit
Shows last 10 admin actions for the chat.

Permissions
- Admin-only commands require you to be a chat admin (or be in BOT_ADMIN_IDS).
- Some moderation actions require the bot to have admin permissions.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Telegram bot token from BotFather.

2) MONGODB_URI (required)
MongoDB connection string.

3) BOT_ADMIN_IDS (optional)
Comma-separated Telegram user IDs with global override admin access.

4) LOG_LEVEL (optional)
Default: info. Supports: debug, info, warn, error.

Troubleshooting
1) Bot not responding
- Confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
- If deployed, ensure only one instance is polling (409 errors will auto-retry).

2) DB errors
- Confirm MONGODB_URI is set and reachable.
- Check logs for collection and operation names.

3) Welcome messages not sending
- Ensure /welcome_on is enabled in the chat.
- Ensure the bot has permission to send messages in the group.

README.md.content:
This is a Telegram community onboarding and admin tools bot built with grammY.

It supports member-facing tools in DMs (rules, FAQ, suggestions) and group features (welcome messages, moderation helpers, announcements).

Quick start
1) Create a Telegram bot with BotFather and get TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
2) Set env vars (see .env.sample)
3) Install and run:
   npm run build
   npm start

Notes
- MongoDB is required for persistence (rules, welcome templates, FAQ, suggestions, audit logs).
- The bot uses long polling via @grammyjs/runner.

See DOCS.md for full command reference and troubleshooting.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Community Builder Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that helps people start, run, and grow communities inside Telegram groups. It focuses on onboarding new members, establishing rules and roles, capturing introductions, creating lightweight engagement loops (prompts, polls), and providing moderators with clear tooling for content management and member support.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence. It supports multi-group operation, per-group configuration, and robust moderation workflows while keeping privacy, rate limits, and operational reliability in mind.

Product goals:
Primary goals
1) Make it easy to launch a community group: rules, welcome message, onboarding steps, and member introductions.
2) Improve retention and engagement: prompts, recurring posts, polls, simple events, and member discovery.
3) Reduce moderator workload: spam/raid mitigation, reporting, queueing items for review, and quick actions.
4) Provide community health visibility: basic analytics like member join trends, active members, top contributors, and onboarding completion.

Non-goals (initial scope control)
1) Not a full CRM or newsletter platform.
2) Not a marketplace or payments system.
3) Not cross-platform; Telegram-only.

Target users and roles
1) Owners/admins: configure community settings and policies.
2) Moderators: handle reports, approvals, and content actions.
3) Members: onboard, introduce themselves, participate in prompts, and discover community resources.

Success metrics
1) Onboarding completion rate (e.g., introductions posted, rules acknowledged).
2) Moderator time saved (e.g., fewer manual welcomes, fewer spam messages remaining).
3) Engagement lift (responses to prompts/polls, event RSVPs).
4) Reduced spam incidents and faster response time to reports.

High-level architecture:
High-level design
1) Single Node.js service/process running grammY.
2) Webhook or long polling depending on deployment preference; design should support either.
3) MongoDB as the system of record for configuration, members, events, and moderation logs.
4) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for media assets used in welcome posts, templates, and exports (if needed).

Core modules (logical boundaries)
1) Bot runtime
   1) grammY bot instance with middleware pipeline.
   2) Update router by chat type (private vs group/supergroup) and by feature area.
2) Identity and permissions
   1) Resolve Telegram user and chat identities.
   2) Admin/mod role checks using Telegram chat member status plus optional bot-managed role overlays.
3) Community configuration
   1) Per-group settings: welcome templates, rules, onboarding flow, moderation thresholds, feature toggles.
4) Onboarding and engagement
   1) Join flow: greet, rules acknowledgement, intro prompts.
   2) Scheduled prompts and announcements driven by a simple in-process scheduler backed by MongoDB.
5) Moderation and safety
   1) Anti-spam heuristics, raid mode, link restrictions.
   2) Member reports and moderator review queue.
   3) Action logging (who did what, when, and why).
6) Observability and operations
   1) Structured logging with correlation identifiers (chat id, user id, update id).
   2) Error tracking strategy (log aggregation in deployment environment).
   3) Admin diagnostics commands.

Update handling and resilience
1) Idempotency: store processed update ids per bot instance runtime window and/or persist recent update ids per chat to reduce duplicated side effects.
2) Rate limiting: per-chat and per-user throttles to prevent command spam and avoid Telegram API limits.
3) Retries: for transient Telegram API failures with bounded retry policies and clear user feedback when needed.
4) Graceful degradation: if MongoDB is unavailable, the bot should still avoid destructive actions and respond with a temporary service message to admins.

Scheduling approach (single process constraint)
1) Use an in-process scheduler loop that reads due jobs from MongoDB (nextRunAt <= now).
2) Jobs must be idempotent and include a lock with short TTL semantics in MongoDB to avoid duplicate sends in case of restart.
3) Keep jobs small: send message, update nextRunAt, and record outcome.

Privacy boundaries
1) Avoid storing message content unless necessary (e.g., introductions, reports). Prefer storing message ids and metadata.
2) Provide per-community retention settings for logs and member data.
3) Provide export and deletion workflows for admins.

Data model:
Collections overview (MongoDB)
1) communities
   1) chatId (unique), type, title
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) settings: welcomeEnabled, welcomeTemplate, rulesText, rulesMessageId, introEnabled, introPrompt, language, timezone, featureFlags
   4) moderation: raidMode, linkPolicy, spamSensitivity, minAccountAgeDays, verificationMode
   5) roles: adminOverrides (optional), moderatorUserIds (optional)

2) users
   1) userId (unique), username, firstName, lastName
   2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
   3) globalFlags: isBannedGlobal (optional)

3) communitymembers
   1) communityChatId + userId (compound unique)
   2) joinedAt, leftAt (optional)
   3) onboarding: rulesAcceptedAt, introMessageId, introText (optional), onboardingState
   4) moderation: warningsCount, mutedUntil (optional), notes (optional)
   5) activity: lastActiveAt, messageCountApprox (optional)

4) moderationactions
   1) communityChatId, actorUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) actionType (warn, mute, kick, ban, deletemessage, approve, reject)
   3) reason, metadata (messageId, evidenceIds)
   4) createdAt

5) reports
   1) communityChatId, reporterUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) messageId (optional), messageLink (optional if available)
   3) category, description
   4) status (open, inreview, resolved, dismissed)
   5) assignedToUserId (optional), resolutionNote (optional)
   6) createdAt, updatedAt

6) scheduledjobs
   1) communityChatId
   2) jobType (prompt, announcement, poll)
   3) payload (template id, text, options)
   4) schedule: cronLike (optional), intervalSeconds (optional), nextRunAt
   5) lock: lockedUntil, lockedBy
   6) lastRunAt, lastResult (success/failure), failureCount

7) contenttemplates (optional)
   1) templateId, communityChatId (optional for global templates)
   2) type (welcome, prompt, rules)
   3) text, mediaRef (optional)
   4) createdAt, updatedAt

Indexes and retention
1) Ensure compound indexes for communitymembers and time-based indexes for moderationactions, reports, and scheduledjobs.
2) Use TTL indexes for ephemeral data (e.g., job locks, transient verification tokens) and optionally for moderation logs based on community retention configuration.

Object/file storage references
1) Store only references in MongoDB (mediaRef) pointing to object storage or filesystem storage paths.
2) Keep media optional; most features can work text-first.

Key flows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin adds bot to a group and grants required permissions (delete messages, ban users, pin messages if needed).
2) Admin runs /setup in the group.
3) Bot verifies admin status via Telegram chat member status.
4) Bot guides configuration via an interactive flow:
   1) Set welcome message
   2) Set rules text (and optionally pin rules)
   3) Enable introductions and choose intro prompt
   4) Configure moderation defaults (raid mode toggle, link policy)
   5) Enable engagement prompts and choose schedule
5) Bot saves community settings in MongoDB and posts a confirmation summary.

2) Member joins onboarding
1) Bot receives newchatmembers event.
2) If welcome is enabled, bot posts a welcome message and a short CTA:
   1) Read rules
   2) Introduce yourself
3) If verificationMode is enabled, bot may restrict new members temporarily (based on available Telegram permissions) and lift restrictions after a successful interaction.
4) Bot records join event and onboarding state in communitymembers.

3) Rules acknowledgement
1) Member clicks an inline button or uses a private chat command to acknowledge rules.
2) Bot records rulesAcceptedAt.
3) Bot optionally assigns a visible role label through messaging (Telegram does not support custom roles natively) and may lift temporary restrictions.

4) Introductions
1) Bot prompts the member to post an introduction in the group (or collects it in private and reposts to the group with consent).
2) Bot stores intro message id and optional intro text (if configured).
3) Bot marks onboarding complete and optionally sends a friendly follow-up with next steps (resources, channels, weekly prompt schedule).

5) Engagement prompts and polls
1) Admin configures recurring prompts via /prompts.
2) Scheduler picks due jobs and posts prompts/polls into the group.
3) Bot tracks basic engagement metrics: count of replies or poll participation.
4) Admin can pause, reschedule, or post ad-hoc prompts.

6) Reporting and moderation queue
1) Members report content via /report (reply to a message) or via an inline button placed on selected bot posts.
2) Bot creates a report record, acknowledges the reporter, and notifies moderators in a dedicated mod chat (optional) or via direct messages.
3) Moderators review and take action through buttons (delete, warn, mute, ban) depending on bot permissions.
4) Bot executes the action, logs it in moderationactions, and resolves the report.

7) Anti-spam and raid mode
1) Bot applies heuristics on messages from new members:
   1) Too many links
   2) Repeated text
   3) Suspicious account age thresholds (where feasible)
2) If triggered, bot can delete the message and create an internal moderation event.
3) In raid mode, bot applies stricter thresholds and may temporarily limit posting for new joins.
4) Provide clear moderator overrides and audit trail.

8) Admin analytics and health checks
1) /stats shows onboarding completion, join/leave trends, reports volume, moderation actions, and engagement.
2) /diagnostics runs permission checks and highlights misconfiguration (missing delete permission, cannot restrict members, etc.).

9) Data deletion and export
1) /privacy shows what is stored and for how long.
2) Admin can request /export for community configuration and summary stats.
3) Admin can run /purgeoldlogs with a retention window.
4) Respect user deletion requests where applicable by removing user profile fields and keeping only aggregated counts if needed.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, middleware, and conversation-style flows.
3) Environment configuration via process environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for community settings, member states, moderation logs, reports, and scheduled jobs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caches only (rate limit counters, temporary interaction state), with safe fallbacks after restart.

Storage
1) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for optional media assets, exports, and template attachments.
2) MongoDB stores only references/paths and metadata for stored objects.

Scheduling and background work
1) In-process scheduler that polls MongoDB for due jobs and uses MongoDB-based locking to avoid duplicates.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Operations
1) Webhook or long polling; choose based on deployment environment constraints.
2) Structured logs written to stdout/stderr for collection by the hosting environment.
3) Basic health endpoint (if using webhooks) to support uptime checks and to confirm MongoDB connectivity.

Security
1) Secrets stored only in environment variables.
2) Strict validation of callback data and command inputs.
3) Permission checks for all admin/moderator actions against Telegram chat roles and community settings.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent handlers for join events, scheduled sends, and moderation actions.
2) Defensive handling for missing permissions and Telegram API errors.
3) Safe defaults: do not delete user content unless rules are clearly configured and bot permissions are verified.

Performance
1) Keep hot-path operations O(1) per update: minimal DB queries, use indexed lookups.
2) Use in-memory rate limiters to reduce repeated DB reads.
3) Batch analytics updates (e.g., update counters periodically) rather than per message if needed.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Rate limit commands per user and per chat.
2) Validate that callback queries originate from the same community context.
3) Maintain an audit trail for moderator actions.
4) Provide a global allowlist for bot owners to access sensitive diagnostics.

Privacy and compliance posture
1) Data minimization: store only what is required for features.
2) Configurable retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.
3) Clear transparency via /privacy command.
4) Allow per-user data deletion requests where feasible.

Maintainability
1) Modular design with feature flags per community.
2) Versioned settings schema with migrations for MongoDB documents.
3) Clear separation of concerns: configuration, onboarding, moderation, engagement, analytics.

Operational readiness
1) Startup checks: verify TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists and MongoDB is reachable.
2) Admin-facing diagnostics to confirm permissions and configuration.
3) Clear runbooks for common failures: missing permissions, webhook misconfiguration, MongoDB downtime.

Internationalization readiness (optional)
1) Store a per-community language setting.
2) Keep all user-visible strings in a message catalog for future localization.

Bot commands:
• /start — Start the bot in private chat and see available features and privacy info. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show help and links to setup steps for admins and usage tips for members. (usage: /help) [General]
• /setup — Interactive community setup wizard for a group/supergroup. (usage: /setup (run in the group by an admin)) [Admin]
• /settings — View and edit community settings (welcome, rules, intros, moderation). (usage: /settings (group admin only)) [Admin]
• /rules — Show the community rules and how to acknowledge them. (usage: /rules) [Community]
• /introduce — Post an introduction prompt and guide the user through introduction steps. (usage: /introduce (member) or /introduce @user (mod/admin)) [Community]
• /prompts — Manage recurring engagement prompts and polls. (usage: /prompts (admin only)) [Admin]
• /prompt — Send an ad-hoc prompt to spark discussion. (usage: /prompt Your question here (admin/mod only)) [Moderation]
• /report — Report a message to moderators (use as a reply). (usage: /report (reply to a message) [optional reason]) [Safety]
• /modqueue — View open reports and moderation items. (usage: /modqueue (mod/admin only)) [Moderation]
• /warn — Warn a user (reply to a message or specify user). (usage: /warn (reply) [reason] or /warn @user [reason]) [Moderation]
• /mute — Temporarily mute a user (requires restrict permissions). (usage: /mute (reply) 30m [reason] or /mute @user 2h [reason]) [Moderation]
• /ban — Ban a user from the community (requires ban permissions). (usage: /ban (reply) [reason] or /ban @user [reason]) [Moderation]
• /raidmode — Toggle stricter anti-spam controls during raids. (usage: /raidmode on|off (admin only)) [Safety]
• /stats — Show community health metrics and onboarding/engagement summaries. (usage: /stats (admin/mod only)) [Analytics]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored and offer admin/user data actions. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /export — Export community configuration and summary stats for admins. (usage: /export (admin only)) [Admin]
• /diagnostics — Check bot permissions, configuration health, and connectivity status. (usage: /diagnostics (admin only)) [Admin]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary goals
1) Make it easy to launch a community group: rules, welcome message, onboarding steps, and member introductions.
2) Improve retention and engagement: prompts, recurring posts, polls, simple events, and member discovery.
3) Reduce moderator workload: spam/raid mitigation, reporting, queueing items for review, and quick actions.
4) Provide community health visibility: basic analytics like member join trends, active members, top contributors, and onboarding completion.

Non-goals (initial scope control)
1) Not a full CRM or newsletter platform.
2) Not a marketplace or payments system.
3) Not cross-platform; Telegram-only.

Target users and roles
1) Owners/admins: configure community settings and policies.
2) Moderators: handle reports, approvals, and content actions.
3) Members: onboard, introduce themselves, participate in prompts, and discover community resources.

Success metrics
1) Onboarding completion rate (e.g., introductions posted, rules acknowledged).
2) Moderator time saved (e.g., fewer manual welcomes, fewer spam messages remaining).
3) Engagement lift (responses to prompts/polls, event RSVPs).
4) Reduced spam incidents and faster response time to reports.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
High-level design
1) Single Node.js service/process running grammY.
2) Webhook or long polling depending on deployment preference; design should support either.
3) MongoDB as the system of record for configuration, members, events, and moderation logs.
4) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for media assets used in welcome posts, templates, and exports (if needed).

Core modules (logical boundaries)
1) Bot runtime
   1) grammY bot instance with middleware pipeline.
   2) Update router by chat type (private vs group/supergroup) and by feature area.
2) Identity and permissions
   1) Resolve Telegram user and chat identities.
   2) Admin/mod role checks using Telegram chat member status plus optional bot-managed role overlays.
3) Community configuration
   1) Per-group settings: welcome templates, rules, onboarding flow, moderation thresholds, feature toggles.
4) Onboarding and engagement
   1) Join flow: greet, rules acknowledgement, intro prompts.
   2) Scheduled prompts and announcements driven by a simple in-process scheduler backed by MongoDB.
5) Moderation and safety
   1) Anti-spam heuristics, raid mode, link restrictions.
   2) Member reports and moderator review queue.
   3) Action logging (who did what, when, and why).
6) Observability and operations
   1) Structured logging with correlation identifiers (chat id, user id, update id).
   2) Error tracking strategy (log aggregation in deployment environment).
   3) Admin diagnostics commands.

Update handling and resilience
1) Idempotency: store processed update ids per bot instance runtime window and/or persist recent update ids per chat to reduce duplicated side effects.
2) Rate limiting: per-chat and per-user throttles to prevent command spam and avoid Telegram API limits.
3) Retries: for transient Telegram API failures with bounded retry policies and clear user feedback when needed.
4) Graceful degradation: if MongoDB is unavailable, the bot should still avoid destructive actions and respond with a temporary service message to admins.

Scheduling approach (single process constraint)
1) Use an in-process scheduler loop that reads due jobs from MongoDB (nextRunAt <= now).
2) Jobs must be idempotent and include a lock with short TTL semantics in MongoDB to avoid duplicate sends in case of restart.
3) Keep jobs small: send message, update nextRunAt, and record outcome.

Privacy boundaries
1) Avoid storing message content unless necessary (e.g., introductions, reports). Prefer storing message ids and metadata.
2) Provide per-community retention settings for logs and member data.
3) Provide export and deletion workflows for admins.
planSections.dataModel:
Collections overview (MongoDB)
1) communities
   1) chatId (unique), type, title
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) settings: welcomeEnabled, welcomeTemplate, rulesText, rulesMessageId, introEnabled, introPrompt, language, timezone, featureFlags
   4) moderation: raidMode, linkPolicy, spamSensitivity, minAccountAgeDays, verificationMode
   5) roles: adminOverrides (optional), moderatorUserIds (optional)

2) users
   1) userId (unique), username, firstName, lastName
   2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
   3) globalFlags: isBannedGlobal (optional)

3) communitymembers
   1) communityChatId + userId (compound unique)
   2) joinedAt, leftAt (optional)
   3) onboarding: rulesAcceptedAt, introMessageId, introText (optional), onboardingState
   4) moderation: warningsCount, mutedUntil (optional), notes (optional)
   5) activity: lastActiveAt, messageCountApprox (optional)

4) moderationactions
   1) communityChatId, actorUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) actionType (warn, mute, kick, ban, deletemessage, approve, reject)
   3) reason, metadata (messageId, evidenceIds)
   4) createdAt

5) reports
   1) communityChatId, reporterUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) messageId (optional), messageLink (optional if available)
   3) category, description
   4) status (open, inreview, resolved, dismissed)
   5) assignedToUserId (optional), resolutionNote (optional)
   6) createdAt, updatedAt

6) scheduledjobs
   1) communityChatId
   2) jobType (prompt, announcement, poll)
   3) payload (template id, text, options)
   4) schedule: cronLike (optional), intervalSeconds (optional), nextRunAt
   5) lock: lockedUntil, lockedBy
   6) lastRunAt, lastResult (success/failure), failureCount

7) contenttemplates (optional)
   1) templateId, communityChatId (optional for global templates)
   2) type (welcome, prompt, rules)
   3) text, mediaRef (optional)
   4) createdAt, updatedAt

Indexes and retention
1) Ensure compound indexes for communitymembers and time-based indexes for moderationactions, reports, and scheduledjobs.
2) Use TTL indexes for ephemeral data (e.g., job locks, transient verification tokens) and optionally for moderation logs based on community retention configuration.

Object/file storage references
1) Store only references in MongoDB (mediaRef) pointing to object storage or filesystem storage paths.
2) Keep media optional; most features can work text-first.

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot helps communities onboard members and gives admins lightweight management tools.

Behavior by chat type
1) Private chat (DM)
- Member tools: view rules, browse/search FAQ, send suggestions.
- Admin tools: manage FAQs, review suggestions, send announcements to configured target chats.

2) Group/supergroup
- Automatically welcomes new members if welcome is enabled for that chat.
- Admin tools: set rules, set welcome template, toggle welcome, moderation warnings, announcements, audit log.

Public commands
1) /start
- In private: shows welcome message and buttons for Rules, FAQ, Suggestion, Help.
- In groups: tells users to DM the bot for member tools.

2) /help
- Shows categorized commands for members and admins.

Member commands (private)
1) /rules
Shows rules for your current community context.

2) /faq
Shows FAQ list or searches when you provide terms.
Examples:
- /faq
- /faq wallet setup

3) /suggest
Prompts you to send a suggestion as your next message.

Admin commands (groups and private)
1) /admin
Shows admin panel summary for the current chat (in groups) or asks you to select a target chat (in private).

2) /setrules <text>
Sets rules text for the chat. If you omit <text>, the bot will ask you to send the next message as rules within 60 seconds.

3) /setwelcome <template>
Sets welcome message template for the chat. Supports placeholders: {first_name}, {username}, {chat_title}.
If you omit <template>, the bot will ask for the next message within 60 seconds.

4) /welcome_on and /welcome_off
Enable or disable welcome messages for the chat.

5) /faq_add <question> | <answer>
Adds an FAQ entry.

6) /faq_edit <id> <question> | <answer>
Edits an FAQ entry by id.

7) /faq_del <id>
Deletes an FAQ entry.

8) /suggestions
Lists recent suggestions for the current target chat.

9) /suggest_set <id> <new|accepted|rejected|planned|done>
Updates suggestion status.

10) /announce
Guided announcement flow: choose target(s), enter text, confirm send.

11) /announce_targets
Manage announcement target chats (add/remove) for your admin account.

12) /warn (reply)
Increments warn count for the replied user in that chat.

13) /warns (reply)
Shows warn count for the replied user.

14) /resetwarns (reply)
Resets warn count.

15) /audit
Shows last 10 admin actions for the chat.

Permissions
- Admin-only commands require you to be a chat admin (or be in BOT_ADMIN_IDS).
- Some moderation actions require the bot to have admin permissions.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Telegram bot token from BotFather.

2) MONGODB_URI (required)
MongoDB connection string.

3) BOT_ADMIN_IDS (optional)
Comma-separated Telegram user IDs with global override admin access.

4) LOG_LEVEL (optional)
Default: info. Supports: debug, info, warn, error.

Troubleshooting
1) Bot not responding
- Confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
- If deployed, ensure only one instance is polling (409 errors will auto-retry).

2) DB errors
- Confirm MONGODB_URI is set and reachable.
- Check logs for collection and operation names.

3) Welcome messages not sending
- Ensure /welcome_on is enabled in the chat.
- Ensure the bot has permission to send messages in the group.

README.md.content:
This is a Telegram community onboarding and admin tools bot built with grammY.

It supports member-facing tools in DMs (rules, FAQ, suggestions) and group features (welcome messages, moderation helpers, announcements).

Quick start
1) Create a Telegram bot with BotFather and get TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
2) Set env vars (see .env.sample)
3) Install and run:
   npm run build
   npm start

Notes
- MongoDB is required for persistence (rules, welcome templates, FAQ, suggestions, audit logs).
- The bot uses long polling via @grammyjs/runner.

See DOCS.md for full command reference and troubleshooting.

import { cfg } from "./config.js";

export function getBotProfile() {
  const lines = [
    "Purpose: Help Telegram communities onboard members and give admins lightweight tools for rules, welcome messages, FAQs, suggestions, announcements, moderation warnings, and audit logs.",
    "", 
    "Public member features (mostly in private chat): /rules, /faq, /suggest.",
    "Public admin features (groups and some private flows): /admin, /setrules, /setwelcome, /welcome_on, /welcome_off, /announce, /announce_targets, /warn, /warns, /resetwarns, /audit.",
    "", 
    "Key rules:",
    "- Private chat is for member tools and some admin management.",
    "- Groups/supergroups: welcome messages for new members (when enabled) and moderation helpers.",
    "- Admin commands require Telegram chat admin permissions, unless your user ID is in BOT_ADMIN_IDS.",
    "- In groups, /start reminds users to DM the bot for member tools."
  ];

  return lines.join("\n");
}

export function getHelpText() {
  return (
    "Members (private):\n" +
    "/rules\n" +
    "/faq\n" +
    "/suggest\n\n" +
    "Admins (groups/private):\n" +
    "/admin\n" +
    "/setrules\n" +
    "/setwelcome\n" +
    "/welcome_on\n" +
    "/welcome_off\n" +
    "/announce\n" +

    "/warn (reply)\n" +
    "/warns (reply)\n" +
    "/resetwarns (reply)\n" +
    "/audit\n\n" +
    "Admin commands require chat admin permissions."
  );
}

aiMessages.content:
Created a new grammY Telegram bot project with MongoDB persistence for chat settings (rules/welcome), FAQ entries, suggestions, warn tracking, and audit logs. Implemented /start with buttons, /help with categorized commands, welcome-on-join behavior with placeholders, admin-only commands with chat-admin checks plus BOT_ADMIN_IDS override, and a prompt-next-message pattern via a pending-input store. Added long polling via @grammyjs/runner with webhook clearing, 409 backoff, and production-safe logs. DOCS.md and .env.sample are included.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Community Builder Telegram Bot (grammY, Node.js ESM)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that helps people start, run, and grow communities inside Telegram groups. It focuses on onboarding new members, establishing rules and roles, capturing introductions, creating lightweight engagement loops (prompts, polls), and providing moderators with clear tooling for content management and member support.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence. It supports multi-group operation, per-group configuration, and robust moderation workflows while keeping privacy, rate limits, and operational reliability in mind.

Product goals:
Primary goals
1) Make it easy to launch a community group: rules, welcome message, onboarding steps, and member introductions.
2) Improve retention and engagement: prompts, recurring posts, polls, simple events, and member discovery.
3) Reduce moderator workload: spam/raid mitigation, reporting, queueing items for review, and quick actions.
4) Provide community health visibility: basic analytics like member join trends, active members, top contributors, and onboarding completion.

Non-goals (initial scope control)
1) Not a full CRM or newsletter platform.
2) Not a marketplace or payments system.
3) Not cross-platform; Telegram-only.

Target users and roles
1) Owners/admins: configure community settings and policies.
2) Moderators: handle reports, approvals, and content actions.
3) Members: onboard, introduce themselves, participate in prompts, and discover community resources.

Success metrics
1) Onboarding completion rate (e.g., introductions posted, rules acknowledged).
2) Moderator time saved (e.g., fewer manual welcomes, fewer spam messages remaining).
3) Engagement lift (responses to prompts/polls, event RSVPs).
4) Reduced spam incidents and faster response time to reports.

High-level architecture:
High-level design
1) Single Node.js service/process running grammY.
2) Webhook or long polling depending on deployment preference; design should support either.
3) MongoDB as the system of record for configuration, members, events, and moderation logs.
4) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for media assets used in welcome posts, templates, and exports (if needed).

Core modules (logical boundaries)
1) Bot runtime
   1) grammY bot instance with middleware pipeline.
   2) Update router by chat type (private vs group/supergroup) and by feature area.
2) Identity and permissions
   1) Resolve Telegram user and chat identities.
   2) Admin/mod role checks using Telegram chat member status plus optional bot-managed role overlays.
3) Community configuration
   1) Per-group settings: welcome templates, rules, onboarding flow, moderation thresholds, feature toggles.
4) Onboarding and engagement
   1) Join flow: greet, rules acknowledgement, intro prompts.
   2) Scheduled prompts and announcements driven by a simple in-process scheduler backed by MongoDB.
5) Moderation and safety
   1) Anti-spam heuristics, raid mode, link restrictions.
   2) Member reports and moderator review queue.
   3) Action logging (who did what, when, and why).
6) Observability and operations
   1) Structured logging with correlation identifiers (chat id, user id, update id).
   2) Error tracking strategy (log aggregation in deployment environment).
   3) Admin diagnostics commands.

Update handling and resilience
1) Idempotency: store processed update ids per bot instance runtime window and/or persist recent update ids per chat to reduce duplicated side effects.
2) Rate limiting: per-chat and per-user throttles to prevent command spam and avoid Telegram API limits.
3) Retries: for transient Telegram API failures with bounded retry policies and clear user feedback when needed.
4) Graceful degradation: if MongoDB is unavailable, the bot should still avoid destructive actions and respond with a temporary service message to admins.

Scheduling approach (single process constraint)
1) Use an in-process scheduler loop that reads due jobs from MongoDB (nextRunAt <= now).
2) Jobs must be idempotent and include a lock with short TTL semantics in MongoDB to avoid duplicate sends in case of restart.
3) Keep jobs small: send message, update nextRunAt, and record outcome.

Privacy boundaries
1) Avoid storing message content unless necessary (e.g., introductions, reports). Prefer storing message ids and metadata.
2) Provide per-community retention settings for logs and member data.
3) Provide export and deletion workflows for admins.

Data model:
Collections overview (MongoDB)
1) communities
   1) chatId (unique), type, title
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) settings: welcomeEnabled, welcomeTemplate, rulesText, rulesMessageId, introEnabled, introPrompt, language, timezone, featureFlags
   4) moderation: raidMode, linkPolicy, spamSensitivity, minAccountAgeDays, verificationMode
   5) roles: adminOverrides (optional), moderatorUserIds (optional)

2) users
   1) userId (unique), username, firstName, lastName
   2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
   3) globalFlags: isBannedGlobal (optional)

3) communitymembers
   1) communityChatId + userId (compound unique)
   2) joinedAt, leftAt (optional)
   3) onboarding: rulesAcceptedAt, introMessageId, introText (optional), onboardingState
   4) moderation: warningsCount, mutedUntil (optional), notes (optional)
   5) activity: lastActiveAt, messageCountApprox (optional)

4) moderationactions
   1) communityChatId, actorUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) actionType (warn, mute, kick, ban, deletemessage, approve, reject)
   3) reason, metadata (messageId, evidenceIds)
   4) createdAt

5) reports
   1) communityChatId, reporterUserId, targetUserId (optional)
   2) messageId (optional), messageLink (optional if available)
   3) category, description
   4) status (open, inreview, resolved, dismissed)
   5) assignedToUserId (optional), resolutionNote (optional)
   6) createdAt, updatedAt

6) scheduledjobs
   1) communityChatId
   2) jobType (prompt, announcement, poll)
   3) payload (template id, text, options)
   4) schedule: cronLike (optional), intervalSeconds (optional), nextRunAt
   5) lock: lockedUntil, lockedBy
   6) lastRunAt, lastResult (success/failure), failureCount

7) contenttemplates (optional)
   1) templateId, communityChatId (optional for global templates)
   2) type (welcome, prompt, rules)
   3) text, mediaRef (optional)
   4) createdAt, updatedAt

Indexes and retention
1) Ensure compound indexes for communitymembers and time-based indexes for moderationactions, reports, and scheduledjobs.
2) Use TTL indexes for ephemeral data (e.g., job locks, transient verification tokens) and optionally for moderation logs based on community retention configuration.

Object/file storage references
1) Store only references in MongoDB (mediaRef) pointing to object storage or filesystem storage paths.
2) Keep media optional; most features can work text-first.

Key flows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin adds bot to a group and grants required permissions (delete messages, ban users, pin messages if needed).
2) Admin runs /setup in the group.
3) Bot verifies admin status via Telegram chat member status.
4) Bot guides configuration via an interactive flow:
   1) Set welcome message
   2) Set rules text (and optionally pin rules)
   3) Enable introductions and choose intro prompt
   4) Configure moderation defaults (raid mode toggle, link policy)
   5) Enable engagement prompts and choose schedule
5) Bot saves community settings in MongoDB and posts a confirmation summary.

2) Member joins onboarding
1) Bot receives newchatmembers event.
2) If welcome is enabled, bot posts a welcome message and a short CTA:
   1) Read rules
   2) Introduce yourself
3) If verificationMode is enabled, bot may restrict new members temporarily (based on available Telegram permissions) and lift restrictions after a successful interaction.
4) Bot records join event and onboarding state in communitymembers.

3) Rules acknowledgement
1) Member clicks an inline button or uses a private chat command to acknowledge rules.
2) Bot records rulesAcceptedAt.
3) Bot optionally assigns a visible role label through messaging (Telegram does not support custom roles natively) and may lift temporary restrictions.

4) Introductions
1) Bot prompts the member to post an introduction in the group (or collects it in private and reposts to the group with consent).
2) Bot stores intro message id and optional intro text (if configured).
3) Bot marks onboarding complete and optionally sends a friendly follow-up with next steps (resources, channels, weekly prompt schedule).

5) Engagement prompts and polls
1) Admin configures recurring prompts via /prompts.
2) Scheduler picks due jobs and posts prompts/polls into the group.
3) Bot tracks basic engagement metrics: count of replies or poll participation.
4) Admin can pause, reschedule, or post ad-hoc prompts.

6) Reporting and moderation queue
1) Members report content via /report (reply to a message) or via an inline button placed on selected bot posts.
2) Bot creates a report record, acknowledges the reporter, and notifies moderators in a dedicated mod chat (optional) or via direct messages.
3) Moderators review and take action through buttons (delete, warn, mute, ban) depending on bot permissions.
4) Bot executes the action, logs it in moderationactions, and resolves the report.

7) Anti-spam and raid mode
1) Bot applies heuristics on messages from new members:
   1) Too many links
   2) Repeated text
   3) Suspicious account age thresholds (where feasible)
2) If triggered, bot can delete the message and create an internal moderation event.
3) In raid mode, bot applies stricter thresholds and may temporarily limit posting for new joins.
4) Provide clear moderator overrides and audit trail.

8) Admin analytics and health checks
1) /stats shows onboarding completion, join/leave trends, reports volume, moderation actions, and engagement.
2) /diagnostics runs permission checks and highlights misconfiguration (missing delete permission, cannot restrict members, etc.).

9) Data deletion and export
1) /privacy shows what is stored and for how long.
2) Admin can request /export for community configuration and summary stats.
3) Admin can run /purgeoldlogs with a retention window.
4) Respect user deletion requests where applicable by removing user profile fields and keeping only aggregated counts if needed.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, middleware, and conversation-style flows.
3) Environment configuration via process environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for community settings, member states, moderation logs, reports, and scheduled jobs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caches only (rate limit counters, temporary interaction state), with safe fallbacks after restart.

Storage
1) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for optional media assets, exports, and template attachments.
2) MongoDB stores only references/paths and metadata for stored objects.

Scheduling and background work
1) In-process scheduler that polls MongoDB for due jobs and uses MongoDB-based locking to avoid duplicates.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Operations
1) Webhook or long polling; choose based on deployment environment constraints.
2) Structured logs written to stdout/stderr for collection by the hosting environment.
3) Basic health endpoint (if using webhooks) to support uptime checks and to confirm MongoDB connectivity.

Security
1) Secrets stored only in environment variables.
2) Strict validation of callback data and command inputs.
3) Permission checks for all admin/moderator actions against Telegram chat roles and community settings.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent handlers for join events, scheduled sends, and moderation actions.
2) Defensive handling for missing permissions and Telegram API errors.
3) Safe defaults: do not delete user content unless rules are clearly configured and bot permissions are verified.

Performance
1) Keep hot-path operations O(1) per update: minimal DB queries, use indexed lookups.
2) Use in-memory rate limiters to reduce repeated DB reads.
3) Batch analytics updates (e.g., update counters periodically) rather than per message if needed.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Rate limit commands per user and per chat.
2) Validate that callback queries originate from the same community context.
3) Maintain an audit trail for moderator actions.
4) Provide a global allowlist for bot owners to access sensitive diagnostics.

Privacy and compliance posture
1) Data minimization: store only what is required for features.
2) Configurable retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.
3) Clear transparency via /privacy command.
4) Allow per-user data deletion requests where feasible.

Maintainability
1) Modular design with feature flags per community.
2) Versioned settings schema with migrations for MongoDB documents.
3) Clear separation of concerns: configuration, onboarding, moderation, engagement, analytics.

Operational readiness
1) Startup checks: verify TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists and MongoDB is reachable.
2) Admin-facing diagnostics to confirm permissions and configuration.
3) Clear runbooks for common failures: missing permissions, webhook misconfiguration, MongoDB downtime.

Internationalization readiness (optional)
1) Store a per-community language setting.
2) Keep all user-visible strings in a message catalog for future localization.

Bot commands:
• /start — Start the bot in private chat and see available features and privacy info. (usage: /start) [General]
• /help — Show help and links to setup steps for admins and usage tips for members. (usage: /help) [General]
• /setup — Interactive community setup wizard for a group/supergroup. (usage: /setup (run in the group by an admin)) [Admin]
• /settings — View and edit community settings (welcome, rules, intros, moderation). (usage: /settings (group admin only)) [Admin]
• /rules — Show the community rules and how to acknowledge them. (usage: /rules) [Community]
• /introduce — Post an introduction prompt and guide the user through introduction steps. (usage: /introduce (member) or /introduce @user (mod/admin)) [Community]
• /prompts — Manage recurring engagement prompts and polls. (usage: /prompts (admin only)) [Admin]
• /prompt — Send an ad-hoc prompt to spark discussion. (usage: /prompt Your question here (admin/mod only)) [Moderation]
• /report — Report a message to moderators (use as a reply). (usage: /report (reply to a message) [optional reason]) [Safety]
• /modqueue — View open reports and moderation items. (usage: /modqueue (mod/admin only)) [Moderation]
• /warn — Warn a user (reply to a message or specify user). (usage: /warn (reply) [reason] or /warn @user [reason]) [Moderation]
• /mute — Temporarily mute a user (requires restrict permissions). (usage: /mute (reply) 30m [reason] or /mute @user 2h [reason]) [Moderation]
• /ban — Ban a user from the community (requires ban permissions). (usage: /ban (reply) [reason] or /ban @user [reason]) [Moderation]
• /raidmode — Toggle stricter anti-spam controls during raids. (usage: /raidmode on|off (admin only)) [Safety]
• /stats — Show community health metrics and onboarding/engagement summaries. (usage: /stats (admin/mod only)) [Analytics]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored and offer admin/user data actions. (usage: /privacy) [General]
• /export — Export community configuration and summary stats for admins. (usage: /export (admin only)) [Admin]
• /diagnostics — Check bot permissions, configuration health, and connectivity status. (usage: /diagnostics (admin only)) [Admin]

Indexes and retention
1) Ensure compound indexes for communitymembers and time-based indexes for moderationactions, reports, and scheduledjobs.
2) Use TTL indexes for ephemeral data (e.g., job locks, transient verification tokens) and optionally for moderation logs based on community retention configuration.

Object/file storage references
1) Store only references in MongoDB (mediaRef) pointing to object storage or filesystem storage paths.
2) Keep media optional; most features can work text-first.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin adds bot to a group and grants required permissions (delete messages, ban users, pin messages if needed).
2) Admin runs /setup in the group.
3) Bot verifies admin status via Telegram chat member status.
4) Bot guides configuration via an interactive flow:
   1) Set welcome message
   2) Set rules text (and optionally pin rules)
   3) Enable introductions and choose intro prompt
   4) Configure moderation defaults (raid mode toggle, link policy)
   5) Enable engagement prompts and choose schedule
5) Bot saves community settings in MongoDB and posts a confirmation summary.

2) Member joins onboarding
1) Bot receives newchatmembers event.
2) If welcome is enabled, bot posts a welcome message and a short CTA:
   1) Read rules
   2) Introduce yourself
3) If verificationMode is enabled, bot may restrict new members temporarily (based on available Telegram permissions) and lift restrictions after a successful interaction.
4) Bot records join event and onboarding state in communitymembers.

3) Rules acknowledgement
1) Member clicks an inline button or uses a private chat command to acknowledge rules.
2) Bot records rulesAcceptedAt.
3) Bot optionally assigns a visible role label through messaging (Telegram does not support custom roles natively) and may lift temporary restrictions.

4) Introductions
1) Bot prompts the member to post an introduction in the group (or collects it in private and reposts to the group with consent).
2) Bot stores intro message id and optional intro text (if configured).
3) Bot marks onboarding complete and optionally sends a friendly follow-up with next steps (resources, channels, weekly prompt schedule).

5) Engagement prompts and polls
1) Admin configures recurring prompts via /prompts.
2) Scheduler picks due jobs and posts prompts/polls into the group.
3) Bot tracks basic engagement metrics: count of replies or poll participation.
4) Admin can pause, reschedule, or post ad-hoc prompts.

6) Reporting and moderation queue
1) Members report content via /report (reply to a message) or via an inline button placed on selected bot posts.
2) Bot creates a report record, acknowledges the reporter, and notifies moderators in a dedicated mod chat (optional) or via direct messages.
3) Moderators review and take action through buttons (delete, warn, mute, ban) depending on bot permissions.
4) Bot executes the action, logs it in moderationactions, and resolves the report.

7) Anti-spam and raid mode
1) Bot applies heuristics on messages from new members:
   1) Too many links
   2) Repeated text
   3) Suspicious account age thresholds (where feasible)
2) If triggered, bot can delete the message and create an internal moderation event.
3) In raid mode, bot applies stricter thresholds and may temporarily limit posting for new joins.
4) Provide clear moderator overrides and audit trail.

8) Admin analytics and health checks
1) /stats shows onboarding completion, join/leave trends, reports volume, moderation actions, and engagement.
2) /diagnostics runs permission checks and highlights misconfiguration (missing delete permission, cannot restrict members, etc.).

9) Data deletion and export
1) /privacy shows what is stored and for how long.
2) Admin can request /export for community configuration and summary stats.
3) Admin can run /purgeoldlogs with a retention window.
4) Respect user deletion requests where applicable by removing user profile fields and keeping only aggregated counts if needed.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot updates, middleware, and conversation-style flows.
3) Environment configuration via process environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for community settings, member states, moderation logs, reports, and scheduled jobs.
2) In-memory maps for short-lived caches only (rate limit counters, temporary interaction state), with safe fallbacks after restart.

Storage
1) Object storage or simple filesystem storage for optional media assets, exports, and template attachments.
2) MongoDB stores only references/paths and metadata for stored objects.

Scheduling and background work
1) In-process scheduler that polls MongoDB for due jobs and uses MongoDB-based locking to avoid duplicates.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Operations
1) Webhook or long polling; choose based on deployment environment constraints.
2) Structured logs written to stdout/stderr for collection by the hosting environment.
3) Basic health endpoint (if using webhooks) to support uptime checks and to confirm MongoDB connectivity.

Security
1) Secrets stored only in environment variables.
2) Strict validation of callback data and command inputs.
3) Permission checks for all admin/moderator actions against Telegram chat roles and community settings.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent handlers for join events, scheduled sends, and moderation actions.
2) Defensive handling for missing permissions and Telegram API errors.
3) Safe defaults: do not delete user content unless rules are clearly configured and bot permissions are verified.

Performance
1) Keep hot-path operations O(1) per update: minimal DB queries, use indexed lookups.
2) Use in-memory rate limiters to reduce repeated DB reads.
3) Batch analytics updates (e.g., update counters periodically) rather than per message if needed.

Security and abuse prevention
1) Rate limit commands per user and per chat.
2) Validate that callback queries originate from the same community context.
3) Maintain an audit trail for moderator actions.
4) Provide a global allowlist for bot owners to access sensitive diagnostics.

Privacy and compliance posture
1) Data minimization: store only what is required for features.
2) Configurable retention: auto-delete logs older than N days.
3) Clear transparency via /privacy command.
4) Allow per-user data deletion requests where feasible.

Maintainability
1) Modular design with feature flags per community.
2) Versioned settings schema with migrations for MongoDB documents.
3) Clear separation of concerns: configuration, onboarding, moderation, engagement, analytics.

Operational readiness
1) Startup checks: verify TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists and MongoDB is reachable.
2) Admin-facing diagnostics to confirm permissions and configuration.
3) Clear runbooks for common failures: missing permissions, webhook misconfiguration, MongoDB downtime.

Internationalization readiness (optional)
1) Store a per-community language setting.
2) Keep all user-visible strings in a message catalog for future localization.
